{{Chess Opening Theory/Position|= |Blackmar-Diemer Gambit (BDG)| |rd|nd|bd|qd|kd|bd|nd|rd|= |pd|pd|pd| |pd|pd|pd|pd|= | | | | | | | | |= | | | | | | | | |= | | | |pl|pd| | | |= | | | | | | | | |= |pl|pl|pl| | |pl|pl|pl|= |rl|nl|bl|ql|kl|bl|nl|rl|= || }} = Blackmar-Diemer Gambit (BDG) Accepted = ===2...dxe4=== White has given up his king's pawn, in hopes of an aggressive game. Since there is no way White can win a pawn back immediately, he can expect this set-up most times he plays the Blackmar-Diemar Gambit. The best move for White would be [[/3. Nc3|Nc3]], putting pressure on the e4-pawn and preparing the move f3, as 3. f3 is well met by 3...e5!, with an edge for Black. One example: 4. dxe5 (other moves are even worse, for example 4. fxe4 Qh4+ and Qxe4 with an extra pawn and an attack, or d5 Nf6 c4 c6 when d5 will soon fall) Qxd1+ Kxd1 Nc6 fxe4 (f4 Bg4+ Ne2 O-O-O+ with a winning attack) Nxe5 with an obvious advantage. Black normally plays 3...Nf6, though 3...f5 and 3...Bf5 are interesting ways to avoid mainline theory. Now, if f3 e5?, dxe5 gains a tempo on the knight. Sometimes [[/3. Bc4|Bc4]] is played, to hopefully trap Black quickly with a variation of [[Chess/Scholar's mate|Scholar's Mate]]. ==Theory table== {{Chess Opening Theory/Table}}. '''1. d4 d5 2. e4 dxe4'''
3 4 5
[[/3. Nc3|Nc3]]
Nf6		 f3
exf3		 Nxf3 ⩱
